title: Debug: OpenAPI conversion limits
task: The OpenAPI experimental feature sometimes fails to convert endpoints. What are the known limitations? Do NOT make any code changes.
output:
Here are the known limitations found in the code, based on reading the implementation directly:

---

### External `$ref` references (`schemas.py:102-107`, `parser.py:163`)
Any `$ref` that doesn't start with `#/` (i.e., external file or URL references like `"./models.yaml#/Foo"`) raises a `ValueError` that propagates up and causes the entire endpoint to fail conversion. All schema definitions must be inlined in the same OpenAPI document.

### Cookie parameters silently dropped (`director.py:114-125`, `models.py:13`)
The parser correctly identifies and maps `cookie` parameters, but `RequestDirector._unflatten_arguments` only handles `path`, `query`, `header`, and `body`. Cookie-located parameters hit the `else: logger.warning(...)` branch and are never included in the outgoing request.

### Request body always sent as JSON (`director.py:60-68`)
`RequestDirector.build()` unconditionally uses `httpx.Request(..., json=json_body)`. Endpoints that only accept `multipart/form-data` or `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` will receive requests with `Content-Type: application/json`, causing failures.

### Only the first content type of request body is used (`schemas.py:242`)
When a request body defines multiple content types, `next(iter(route.request_body.content_schema))` takes whichever comes first. If a spec lists `application/xml` before `application/json`, the XML schema is used for the flat parameter model.

### `allOf` in request body only merges `properties` and `required` (`schemas.py:255-278`)
When a request body schema uses `allOf`, sub-schema `properties` and `required` fields are merged into a flat object — but all other keywords (`additionalProperties`, constraints, etc.) in those sub-schemas are discarded.

### Only the primary success response is extracted (`parser.py:414-435`)
The parser picks one success response in priority order: 200 → 201 → 202 → 204 → 207 → any 2xx. All error responses (4xx, 5xx) and `default` responses are completely ignored — no error response schema is available.

### Non-property/non-object request bodies use the schema title as the parameter name (`schemas.py:371-385`)
For bodies that aren't objects with `properties` (e.g., bare arrays or primitives), the flat parameter name is derived from `schema.title.lower()`, with a fallback to `"body_data"`. This can produce unintuitive parameter names.

### `_replace_ref_with_defs` has a mutual-exclusion bug for complex schemas (`schemas.py:96-128`)
The function uses an `if $ref / elif properties / elif items` chain. If a schema has a top-level `$ref` and also contains `properties` (unusual but valid), only the `$ref` is rewritten and nested property schemas are not recursed into, potentially leaving unconverted `#/components/schemas/` refs.

### OpenAPI 2.0 / Swagger not supported (`parser.py:63-86`)
Only `3.0.x` and `3.1.x` are handled. Non-3.x versions are passed to the OpenAPI 3.1 `model_validate()` and will raise a `ValidationError`.

### No streaming support
Streaming API responses are not handled — noted as a planned future enhancement in the README.

claudeJson.result:
Here are the known limitations found in the code, based on reading the implementation directly:

---

### External `$ref` references (`schemas.py:102-107`, `parser.py:163`)
Any `$ref` that doesn't start with `#/` (i.e., external file or URL references like `"./models.yaml#/Foo"`) raises a `ValueError` that propagates up and causes the entire endpoint to fail conversion. All schema definitions must be inlined in the same OpenAPI document.

### Cookie parameters silently dropped (`director.py:114-125`, `models.py:13`)
The parser correctly identifies and maps `cookie` parameters, but `RequestDirector._unflatten_arguments` only handles `path`, `query`, `header`, and `body`. Cookie-located parameters hit the `else: logger.warning(...)` branch and are never included in the outgoing request.

### Request body always sent as JSON (`director.py:60-68`)
`RequestDirector.build()` unconditionally uses `httpx.Request(..., json=json_body)`. Endpoints that only accept `multipart/form-data` or `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` will receive requests with `Content-Type: application/json`, causing failures.

### Only the first content type of request body is used (`schemas.py:242`)
When a request body defines multiple content types, `next(iter(route.request_body.content_schema))` takes whichever comes first. If a spec lists `application/xml` before `application/json`, the XML schema is used for the flat parameter model.

### `allOf` in request body only merges `properties` and `required` (`schemas.py:255-278`)
When a request body schema uses `allOf`, sub-schema `properties` and `required` fields are merged into a flat object — but all other keywords (`additionalProperties`, constraints, etc.) in those sub-schemas are discarded.

### Only the primary success response is extracted (`parser.py:414-435`)
The parser picks one success response in priority order: 200 → 201 → 202 → 204 → 207 → any 2xx. All error responses (4xx, 5xx) and `default` responses are completely ignored — no error response schema is available.

### Non-property/non-object request bodies use the schema title as the parameter name (`schemas.py:371-385`)
For bodies that aren't objects with `properties` (e.g., bare arrays or primitives), the flat parameter name is derived from `schema.title.lower()`, with a fallback to `"body_data"`. This can produce unintuitive parameter names.

### `_replace_ref_with_defs` has a mutual-exclusion bug for complex schemas (`schemas.py:96-128`)
The function uses an `if $ref / elif properties / elif items` chain. If a schema has a top-level `$ref` and also contains `properties` (unusual but valid), only the `$ref` is rewritten and nested property schemas are not recursed into, potentially leaving unconverted `#/components/schemas/` refs.

### OpenAPI 2.0 / Swagger not supported (`parser.py:63-86`)
Only `3.0.x` and `3.1.x` are handled. Non-3.x versions are passed to the OpenAPI 3.1 `model_validate()` and will raise a `ValidationError`.

### No streaming support
Streaming API responses are not handled — noted as a planned future enhancement in the README.
